text:
I have exciting news for prospective MBAs looking to enter the world of management consulting upon graduation. McKinsey & Company has announced two fantastic programs that can help make that happen for you - McKinsey Early Access and Inspire: Black, Hispanic & Latino in Business, our diversity program."
""
"I can attest to the immense quality of my peers who have gone through these programs and the corresponding benefit it has had for their careers. I can also vouch for the fair and broadminded recruiting practices at McKinsey, so don't count yourself out before taking the time to learn more. "
""
"Should you have any questions about the Firm, our Marketing & Sales practice, or joining the New York office, I would be delighted to have a chat or, indeed, connect you with others who are equally well-versed in other offices and specialties."
""
"Please review the details of both programs below and keep their respective deadlines in mind. Best of luck!"
""
"2022 McKinsey Early Access:"
"McKinsey Early Access introduces you to McKinsey and consulting before you start business school. As part of the program, you will connect with peers in the US and build relationships with our consultants in advance of the fall recruiting season, start of classes and clubs, etc. McKinsey Early Access is inclusive to all backgrounds and helps support you as you start your MBA experience. We are particularly excited to meet problem solvers who are entrepreneurial, creative, and eager to participate in a close-knit, diverse, mentorship-focused community. "
""
"The application deadline is May 20, 2022. "
""
"You can find out more about the program on our website: https://lnkd.in/dW3ir5ni "
" "
"2022 Inspire: Black, Hispanic & Latino in Business:"
"We invite you to apply to Inspire, a two-day in-person event designed to support the professional development of underrepresented groups in leadership roles. Attendees who would like to pursue consulting careers will have the opportunity to jumpstart their McKinsey relationships and recruiting journey. At Inspire, we want you will feel at home, included, and comfortable in asking questions!  Inspire will introduce incoming MBA students in the United States who identify as Black, African-American, Hispanic, Latino/Latina, and/or Native American to our people and consulting. Attendees will hear examples of how we strive to make our firm—and our clients and society at large—more inclusive and equitable. Candidates will also get to meet like-minded peers and McKinsey colleagues in an intimate format. Inspire attendees who are interested in career opportunities at McKinsey will be invited to a special series of interview preparation and connectivity sessions throughout the year. Learn more about the program here: https://lnkd.in/dZx_j_zw. "
""
"Applications are accepted on a rolling basis until May 6th. To apply, visit https://lnkd.in/dt72jcxW
voice_reason: api_error

text:
Ever felt your dream job was completely out of reach? "
"Ever felt completely lost while preparing for it? "
"Ever thought your prep wouldn’t make a difference? "
""
"At the start of my second year of the MBA, I felt all that and more when I decided to apply to McKinsey. I was totally out of my depth because I hadn’t recruited for consulting the previous year, so there was a lot of catching up to do and not much time to do it. "
""
"So I did the only thing I could: I put in dozens of hours to learn everything I could about casing and their interview process and catalog it in painful detail. The byproduct was an only *slightly* intense set of notes that I studied religiously. "
""
"Spoiler alert: I got the job! "
""
"What’s more, those notes that I had shared casually with some classmates became a big hit: they’ve racked up 1600+ views since September 2021 (see screenshot). That’s a lot considering Johnson only has about 200 people applying for consulting jobs across both years. "
""
"In retrospect, it makes total sense why I ended up enjoying getting through that interview process - it’s the same reason why I started The ApplyAI! Getting a great job is far from straightforward, and my team and I are planning to change that. "
""
"That’s why I’m here to share that same resource with LinkedIn. Here’s the McKinsey version (http://bit.ly/3NX5wST) and here’s a Firm-agnostic version (https://bit.ly/48rAbA1) for people looking at places besides the Firm. Please note that these are *supplementary* resources; they aren’t intended to build your capabilities from scratch. Think of them as an additional perspective on casing or behavioral interviews."
""
"If you were one of the people these notes helped, I’d love for you to leave a comment or repost for visibility! "
""
"For everyone else, follow us at The ApplyAI, sign up for our beta waitlist at www.theapply.ai, and watch this space for the even more exciting announcement that’s coming soon. My co-founder Logan and I think that’s wild that job hunters, particularly fresh grads, are expected to just “figure it out” without real help. We’re here to change that. "
""
"Finally, a huge thanks to my peers at Johnson who helped me get one of the biggest opportunities of my life. Here's a non-exhaustive list: Andrew Shin Sebastian Rodriguez Goumashvili Kevin Wang Madhuri Mehta Jacob Lehman Surina Shahani Uma Rajeswari Somireddi Thomas Stevenson Tarun Johnson Deepanshu Mehta José Enrique Gutiérrez Montaño. The camaraderie is what makes our program special, and I’m so grateful to have had the firsthand experience.
voice_reason: Has some genuine personality — the self-deprecating humor ('only *slightly* intense'), specific details (1600+ views, Johnson's 200 applicants), and the conversational directness are real voice markers. But it's fundamentally a LinkedIn post, and it shows: the rhetorical triple-question opening is a LinkedIn template, 'Spoiler alert: I got the job!' is formulaic, and the CTA-heavy closing (follow, sign up, repost) dilutes the voice into marketing copy. The middle section where he tells the actual story is the strongest — specific, warm, human. The bookends are generic LinkedIn engagement bait. Mixed signal overall.

text:
*Pastes in a job description*"
"*Pastes in their resume*"
"*Instructs ChatGPT to “tailor my resume”*"
"*Unknowingly lives to regret it* "
"🤦"
""
"The year is 2025, and if you’ve done anything resembling the above, you’ve unwittingly enlisted yourself in the ChatGPT Resume Clone Army - and martyred your job prospects in the process."
""
"I’ve seen countless people use this tactic and it never works out in their favor. Here’s why."
""
"This approach to resume customization fails miserably because:"
"➡️ ChatGPT is constrained to working with what's already on your resume"
"➡️ It can only swap words around or extend ideas in generic ways"
"➡️ It’s liable to making dangerous assumptions about your experiences, leaving you to waste time reversing those changes at best and getting caught out in an interview at worst"
"➡️ It produces resumes that are broadly indistinguishable and drive recruiters crazy!"
""
"Now let me be blunt: resumes are fundamentally terrible. They're one-dimensional representations of complex, nuanced careers. In an ideal world, they would be as extinct as dial-up internet. But at Careerspan, we’re pragmatists working within a broken system while pushing for better alternatives. And I firmly believe we’ve found one with our one-of-kind approach to resume tailoring."
""
"So what’s the right way to do things? Giving an AI your full backstory to work with - which is what we do 💪"
""
"This means:"
"👉 Starting with AI coaching conversations to uncover your FULL story BEFORE touching the resume"
"👉 Honing in on hidden achievements and forgotten accomplishments"
"👉 Structuring bullets using our proven Action-Impact-Scale-Skills framework"
""
"Our results speak for themselves: a 39% increase in ATS scores compared to original resumes and an 18% improvement over ChatGPT-tailored versions."
""
"This isn't just about swapping keywords like an overpaid thesaurus - it's about semantic optimization that analyzes job descriptions at a conceptual level and maps your capabilities onto requirements through pattern recognition."
""
"That’s why the real value of using Careerspan is getting help with articulating your unique professional story in the greatest amount of detail possible."
""
"So until we overthrow the yoke of resume tyranny altogether, I'm committed to making its burden that much easier."
""
"📣 Check out my full article on our blog in the comments below and like this post if you’re ready to defect from the ChatGPT clone army for good!"
""
"#AIResumes #CareerDevelopment #JobSearch #ResumeStrategy
voice_reason: Strong voice markers throughout: 'martyred your job prospects,' 'overpaid thesaurus,' 'overthrow the yoke of resume tyranny' show dry humor and distinctive phrasing. Direct and assertive ('let me be blunt,' 'resumes are fundamentally terrible'). Backs claims with specific data (39% ATS increase, 18% improvement). The opening narrative device is creative. Docked from 5 because the LinkedIn post format introduces some formulaic elements (emoji bullet lists, hashtag block, CTA) and a few phrases lean slightly promotional ('our results speak for themselves,' 'one-of-a-kind approach') rather than letting the substance fully do the convincing. But the personality is unmistakably present — the irreverence, the specificity, the refusal to hedge.

text:
A brief update for my wonderful peers and colleagues on LinkedIn:"
""
"⏲️ What I’ve been up to"
"I’ve been growing The ApplyAI with my amazing co-founder Logan Currie."
""
"We’re building an AI career companion that’ll help you land your next big opportunity (more on this soon!) but I first wanted to catch everyone up. "
""
"🛫 The origin story"
""
"My first real job was in admissions and career coaching. I grew to love it, and I prided myself on the strong working relationships I built with clients. "
""
"The whole job boils down to two things: Hoining in on the standout things about my clients and helping them convey those highlights in a standout way. "
""
"After 5 years though, I grew restless and sought out new challenges. I loved deeply impacting people's lives, but since machines couldn't coach people at the time, I was never able to do so at scale. "
""
"🚀 How it started"
""
"Fast forward to November 2022, and the commercial launch of ChatGPT got me thinking. "Can Generative AI be used to crack that previously uncrackable nut: coaching at scale?""
""
"I didn't have the answer, but I knew who could help me find out. So, I did what any normal person would do: I prepared a 1000-word memo (read: screed) that sketched out the fundamentals of what we're building today. Then, without batting an eyelid, I sent it to Logan along with an emphatic “we NEED to talk”. The rest, as they say, is history. "
""
"👏 Why we’re in it to win it"
""
"Despite some bumps along the way, our conviction has never been higher. There’s a lot of “pain” being felt by job and promotion seekers right now - it’s harder than ever to get noticed and more competition than ever. "
""
"Why? Most people don’t know how to advocate for themselves. Those do usually benefitted from socioeconomic privilege or innate talent that eased their path. "
""
"We want to give everyone the benefits of a great career coach at a fraction of the cost, which is why we’re all in on this idea!"
""
"🙏 An ask and an offer"
""
"I want to end with an ask and an offer for my LinkedIn community. Both are optional. Either will be well received!"
""
"🗣️ The Ask: if you have strong feelings about jobs, career building, or recruitment and you’re open to sharing your thoughts, please DM me! I absolutely want to hear from you!"
""
"🎁 The Offer: If you or anyone you know needs help landing their next opportunity, put them in touch with me! I’ll personally onboard them onto the tool and give them a career coaching session. Book my time here: https://bit.ly/46TlKmw.
voice_reason: Heavy LinkedIn formulaic patterns undermine voice signal: emoji section headers, 'wonderful peers and colleagues,' 'amazing co-founder,' 'we're all in on this idea!' — classic platform-native fluff. The hedging and softening ('Both are optional. Either will be well received!') contradicts V's direct, assertive style. A few flickers of real voice exist — the 1000-word memo parenthetical '(read: screed)' and 'without batting an eyelid' show personality, and the 'previously uncrackable nut' line has some of V's specificity. But the overall piece reads as LinkedIn announcement template with enthusiasm padding rather than V's warmth-through-substance approach. The origin story section has potential but stays surface-level instead of earning authority through evidence.

text:
"Whatever value they provide for weary job seekers, it’s worth noting that spray-and-pray [AI tools] don’t address the larger issue that prevents many applicants from landing a job” "
""
"Aki Ito really got to the heart of the matter in their Business Insider article on AI Job Search Bots: there’s an applicant arms race going on, where people are applying to more jobs than ever - and by all accounts, hearing back less and less. "
""
"Employers have had an overabundance of candidates long before these spray-and-pray tools emerged - there were an average of 250 applicants per corporate opening in 2023. I wouldn’t be surprised to see that number double in 2024."
""
"So sure, you could splurge on one of these tools, but if you can’t effectively communicate how great of a candidate you are in the first place, it won’t matter how many places you apply to: you’ll be invisible to employers who can’t see the best “you”. "
""
"I call this the “self-advocacy skill gap”.  Basically, how good is someone at putting themselves forward as a great candidate? This can be through their materials, their networking outreach messages, or interview answers. "
""
"In my experience, people are, at most, okay at self-advocacy – they can’t translate the great things about themselves into messaging that’s appealing and accessible to potential employers. The result is a resume or outreach message that looks like it’s coming from only a “just ok” candidate.  And in today’s job market, “just ok” is far from good enough. "
""
"So for those of you struggling to hear back about interviews or make it past the screening call, ask yourselves this: is the version of myself I’m putting out there - in terms of my materials or interview answers - top quartile quality? "
""
"If not, there isn’t a job search bot in the world that can help you until you lock down your messaging and get good at putting it forward."
""
"Luckily, we’re building a tool to help people close their self-advocacy skill gap at The ApplyAI. It’s free for another few months while we test new features, so sign up at www.theapply.ai and check it out. "
""
"P.S. Leave a comment or DM me if you want to be personally onboarded to the tool. I’ll show you exactly how to use our tool to take your job search/career growth journey to the next level."
""
"Like I said, it's a free tool, so I can't make it a sales pitch even if I tired!"
""
"Link to Aki's article: https://lnkd.in/grtkaN9W
voice_reason: Strong voice markers throughout: coined terminology ('self-advocacy skill gap'), specific data points (250 applicants per opening), direct challenge to the reader ('ask yourselves this'), and dry humor ('can't make it a sales pitch even if I tried'). The 'spray-and-pray' framing and 'invisible to employers who can't see the best you' show distinctive thinking. Docked from 5 because the closing CTA softens into slightly generic LinkedIn-post territory ('take your job search/career growth journey to the next level') and the PS feels like standard social selling rather than V's usual substance-first authority. But the core argument structure — evidence first, then reframe the problem, then offer — is distinctly V.

text:
In the 1998 Tour de France, you had to go 18 riders down the finisher's list until you found one that *wasn't* doping."
""
"Was it because most cyclists are bad people?"
""
"While I'm tempted to say so as a New Yorker who's almost been run over a few times, the more honest answer is that they were part of a system that incentivized cheating."
""
"Sound familiar? Today's job market is failing the same way."
""
"When companies filter candidates by the hundreds using blunt instruments like ATS systems and proxies like pedigree, what do you expect candidates to do?"
""
"Juice their applications, GPT their cover letters, and inflate their backgrounds."
""
"Not because they're dishonest—rather, it's because they're being rational when faced with a flawed system, even if they're also inadvertently making things worse for everyone."
""
"Because when trust collapses, companies retreat further to "safe" signals like Ivy degrees and FAANG work experience."
""
"These proxies may signal competence, but competence alone does not a great hire make!"
""
"Put it this way: if you're starting a band, it's not always about finding the most technically gifted instrumentalist; rather, it's about finding someone with whom everyone jives musically."
""
"The best hires can do the job, but also so much more: they're the ones who step up when teammates struggle. Who mentor during coffee breaks. Who herald company values."
""
"That stuff doesn't show up on the resume -- but they are actually the overriding factors in job success. "
""
"46% of hires churn by the 18-month mark. 90% of the reason why is a values, mindset, or soft skills mismatch."
""
"So we have two roads ahead of us as employers."
""
"Path 1: Add more hoops. More tests. More paranoid screening. Watch as candidates get better at gaming those too."
""
"Path 2: Seek validated signals that actually matter - the stuff that can't be faked by ChatGPT or BSed in an interview."
""
"At Careerspan, we're betting everything on making Path 2 viable and scalable."
""
"Because when you assess people on the totality of what they bring to the table, square pegs end up in square holes, and everybody wins.
voice_reason: Textbook V voice. Opens with a specific, unexpected analogy (Tour de France doping) that earns authority through evidence before connecting to career coaching. The dry humor lands perfectly ('tempted to say so as a New Yorker who's almost been run over a few times'). Assertive framing with no hedging ('we're betting everything'). Specific stats deployed for credibility (46% churn, 90% mismatch, 18 riders deep). The band analogy is warmth-through-substance — accessible without being dumbed down. Distinctive rhythmic choices: the inverted syntax ('competence alone does not a great hire make!'), the punchy parallel structure in Path 1/Path 2, the deliberate em-dashes. Professional but deeply human — calls out systemic failure without moralizing, frames candidates as rational actors rather than villains. The closing line ('square pegs end up in square holes') is classic V: takes a familiar idiom and flips it into an optimistic reframe. Strong Careerspan positioning woven throughout without feeling like a pitch. Excellent training signal across multiple voice dimensions.

text:
A dozen Future of Work founders walk into a Zoom room…"
""
"You'd expect: Twelve simultaneous pitches, a battle royale of buzzwords, and at least three people claiming they're 'the Netflix of careers'. "
""
"What actually happened: People... listened? Asked thoughtful questions? Shared high-impact insights like they were recipe tips?"
""
"The diversity was staggering: from a job-finder that actually leverages your network (#LinkedIn, take notes) to a mentor-matching app that fosters real relationships to an interview tool that encourages AI collaboration instead of forbidding it."
""
"It was a privilege bringing together this fantastic group building the next generation of career tech. "
""
"Normally, this would've devolved into competing pitches and "Uber for X" madness. Instead, we had remarkably thoughtful discussions, founders helping competitors debug their GTM, and enough laughs to feel like old friends."
""
"Here's why: We all believe tech has been failing job seekers for too long."
""
"The old guard optimized for efficiency and employers. This crew optimizes for self-advocacy and humanity. The collaboration wasn't performative—it was founders genuinely wanting each other to succeed because the space is big enough when we're solving real problems."
""
"Yes, some monetize through B2B—we do too—but we're building with a radical premise: what if we helped humans showcase their real capabilities instead of reducing them to keyword soup?"
""
"If career tech has felt stale, it's because we've been building for the wrong scorecard. Yesterday proved the tide has already turned."
""
"Thanks for your time and your contributions 🔌 Andy Mowat (Whispered), Ariel Zebaida (StepStone AI), Asher King-Abramson (Warmer Jobs), Daniel Shalinsky (Withe), Dylan Mikus (Fixpoint), Emilio Van Cotthem (Subscription Intern), Fardeen Khimani 😇 (Rounds.so), Henry Xiao (Stealth), JEFF H SIPE, INTERVIEW EXPERT (https://lnkd.in/eY9k_qsQ), Jerry Chen (www.latteconnect.com), Tejas Ravindra (Startery), Logan Currie (Careerspan)"
""
"P.S. If you're a founder or operator in this space and would like to join our next session, DM me!
voice_reason: Textbook V voice. Opens with dry observational humor (the subverted expectations structure), earns authority through specificity (names real products, real people, real differentiators), assertive framing ('tech has been failing job seekers for too long', 'building for the wrong scorecard') without hedging, warmth delivered through substance not platitudes. The parenthetical asides ('(#LinkedIn, take notes)', 'People... listened?') are distinctive rhythm markers. Direct call to action at the end. Zero corporate speak despite being a LinkedIn post about a professional event—that's hard to pull off and very characteristic.

"Yes, some monetize through B2B—we do too—but we're building with a radical premise: what if we helped humans showcase their real capabilities instead of reducing them to keyword soup?"
""
"If career tech has felt stale, it's because we've been building for the wrong scorecard. Yesterday proved the tide has already turned."
""
"Thanks for your time and your contributions 🔌 Andy Mowat (Whispered), Ariel Zebaida (StepStone AI), Asher King-Abramson (Warmer Jobs), Daniel Shalinsky (Withe), Dylan Mikus (Fixpoint), Emilio Van Cotthem (Subscription Intern), Fardeen Khimani 😇 (Rounds.so), Henry Xiao (Stealth), JEFF H SIPE, INTERVIEW EXPERT (https://lnkd.in/eY9k_qsQ), Jerry Chen (www.latteconnect.com), Tejas Ravindra (Startery), Logan Currie (Careerspan)"
""
"P.S. If you're a founder or operator in this space and would like to join our next session, DM me!
voice_reason: This is peak V voice. The opening joke subverts expectations with dry humor ('twelve simultaneous pitches, a battle royale of buzzwords'). It's assertive without hedging ('tech has been failing job seekers for too long'—no 'I think' or 'perhaps'). Authority is earned through specificity—naming real companies, real dynamics, real outcomes rather than vague praise. The 'Netflix of careers' and 'Uber for X' jabs show observational humor rooted in lived experience. 'Keyword soup' and 'wrong scorecard' are distinctive, specific language choices. The warmth comes through substance—he praises people by describing what they actually built, not with empty superlatives. The rhetorical structure (expectation vs. reality, old guard vs. new crew) creates a distinctive rhythm. Even the B2B monetization aside ('yes, some monetize through B2B—we do too') shows transparency that builds trust. This is a strong training signal across almost every dimension of V's voice profile.

text:
As someone who moved to America 15 years ago for college, I've grown to deeply embrace this country, its people, and its values. Receiving my green card this past April was one of my proudest moments - making official what I've felt in my heart for years."
""
"That's why this situation with federal workers being laid off feels so personal to me. Due to political circumstances beyond their control, countless loyal public servants are being thrust into the most uncertain job market in recent memory. "
""
"I know full well the pressures of making a career transition, so I can appreciate the magnitude of the challenge facing federal workers forced to shift from the public to the private sector during an already stressful time. It's a uniquely difficult switch that threatens to leave tens of thousands of careers - and lives - in limbo. "
""
"I committed my future to this country because I believe in what America can be at its best - a place where we build each other up and rise together. "
""
"That's why I'm proud to announce that Careerspan will be offering free pro access ($49 for a 3-month license) to all federal workers affected by these layoffs. "
""
"No verification needed — we're operating on the honor system because bureaucracy is the last thing you need right now."
""
"Our platform helps you:"
"• Map your government experience to any private sector role"
"• Develop talking points that resonate with hiring managers"
"• Create materials that get you cut through in competitive hiring processes"
"• Build confidence in your ability to make this transition successfully"
""
"The skills you've developed serving our country are invaluable—they just need to be framed in ways private employers understand. Consider us unequivocally on your side in your effort to do that. "
""
"Signup link in comments. Please consider sharing or reposting to support your fellow Americans. "
""
"#FederalWorkers #PublicService #JobSearch
voice_reason: Strong personal voice with several distinctive markers: the immigration narrative is specific and authentic, 'bureaucracy is the last thing you need right now' is classic dry humor with warmth, 'unequivocally on your side' is assertive without hedging, and the honor system detail shows substance-over-process thinking. Minor deductions: some phrases lean slightly generic LinkedIn ('rise together,' 'invaluable'), and the bullet points are functional but could be sharper. Overall a good training signal — the personal stakes, direct offer, and tonal shifts from vulnerable to assertive are clearly V's voice.

text:
I had a fantastic afternoon last Friday sharing the stage with Mike Mendola from PwC and Emma (Vites) Patel from LinkedIn—with the brilliant Julia Munslow from The Wall Street Journal moderating—on how AI can help your career thrive. It was the perfect mix of perspectives — drawing from the best insights that industry professionals, career practitioners, and networking experts can offer."
""
"Credit to the students who attended; they were absolutely relentless (in the best way!) with their questions about staying relevant, job hunting effectively, and maintaining mental resilience in this rapidly evolving landscape. Amidst the doom-and-gloom that dominates AI conversations, it was wonderful to see a group of young people who were not only open to embracing AI but eager to be at the vanguard of the change it’s bringing about."
""
"For those who asked about my go-to’s for learning about AI, here’s a small sample:"
"For practical explainers: Tina Huang (YT)"
"For deeper tech dives: 3Blue1Brown (YT) and Uncoverage (YT)"
"For news and trends: AI Search (YT), Every.to (Newsletter)"
"For cutting-edge conversations: Dwarkesh Patel (Podcast)"
"For a sample of diverse perspectives: Andrew Ng (@AndrewYNg), Aaron Levie (@levie), Deedy Das (@deedy), Lenny Rachitsky (@lennysan), Pieter Levels (@levelsio), and Aidan McLaughlin (@aidan_mclau)(X/Bluesky)"
""
"Having been to a few of Kamina’s events now, I can say with confidence that they deserve the rave reviews they get. HumanUptions creates spaces where students can directly engage with industry professionals about their future, which isn't just nice — it's invaluable. I also know its greatly appreciated by all."
""
"P.S. My cofounder Logan Currie and I are considering launching a podcast focused on leveraging AI for career advancement. Who would be your dream guest? Drop their name below and we'll see what magic we can work!
voice_reason: Mixed signal. Opens with generic LinkedIn-post conventions ('fantastic afternoon,' 'perfect mix of perspectives,' 'brilliant Julia Munslow') that feel formulaic. But voice emerges in spots: 'absolutely relentless (in the best way!)' shows warmth-through-substance, the curated resource list with specific platform tags is characteristically direct and useful, and 'isn't just nice — it's invaluable' has assertive rhythm. The 'doom-and-gloom' / 'vanguard of change' phrasing leans corporate-adjacent. The P.S. podcast teaser is standard engagement-bait. Overall: genuine enthusiasm bleeds through but the LinkedIn-post frame dampens the distinctive voice markers. Decent training signal for tone and structure, but diluted by platform conventions.

text:
🚨 Warning: This is NOT career advice. It's advice about career advice."
""
"Ever notice how career guidance can feel like a maze of contradictions? One expert says "job hop every two years," another insists "show loyalty.""
""
"Both can't be right... or can they?"
""
"Here's what I've learned after years of career coaching: Most folks haven't cultivated "advice literacy" - the skill of understanding why certain guidance exists and how to apply it to our unique situations."
""
"All conventional wisdom has implicit assumptions baked into it."
""
"For example, when someone says "follow your passion," they're assuming you have clarity about what that is, financial runway to pursue it, and that you're capable of translating passion into career success. "
""
"But what if you're supporting a family, discovering your interests through work, or building passion through mastery? "
""
"The advice isn't wrong - you need to understand it's really about finding work that energizes you, which can happen in many ways."
""
"Developing advice literacy means:"
"⚫️ Understanding the context behind guidance"
"⚫️ Recognizing what assumptions it makes about your situation"
"⚫️ Adapting the core insight to fit your actual constraints"
""
"This skill transforms how you level up. Instead of feeling confused by contradictory advice, you start seeing patterns."
""
"You realize that seemingly opposite guidance often targets different contexts, career stages, or goals."
""
"And once you develop this lens, every piece of career content becomes more valuable--even when it isn't immediately relevant."
""
"Moral of the story: Stop blindly collecting advice and start thoughtfully incorporating it into a mental model."
""
"🧐 What contradictory career advice has left you scratching your head? Let's see if we can resolve the paradox in the comments!
voice_reason: Has some genuine voice markers — the 'advice literacy' concept is specific and original, and the framework thinking (implicit assumptions, context-dependent guidance) reflects real expertise. But the execution leans LinkedIn-generic: emoji hooks (🚨🧐), rhetorical questions as structure ('Both can't be right... or can they?'), bullet-point listicles, and a 'let's discuss in the comments!' CTA. The warmth-through-substance is partially there but diluted by formulaic social media scaffolding. 'Most folks haven't cultivated' and 'here's what I've learned' are softened hedges that don't match the assertive-without-hedging profile. The core insight is genuinely V's — the packaging isn't.

text:
Today, Careerspan sets off on a journey to redefine early-career talent evaluation through our new partnership with Startery."
""
"Startery already stands apart as one of the most thoughtfully designed entry points into startup ecosystems—structured, intentional, and deeply values-driven."
""
"Our shared premise is simple but powerful: resumes alone can't capture what makes someone truly exceptional, especially for early-career roles. You need to know the person behind the page. "
""
"Fortunately, assessing mentality, values, and soft skills—the intangible qualities that are far more accurate predictors of performance and fit—is precisely where Careerspan excels."
""
"Going forward, we’ll be powering Startery’s evaluation layer—helping identify standout talent not just by what they’ve done, but also by how they think, what they care about, and the way they approach challenges."
""
"This integration will become foundational to Startery’s Launchpad, in time evolving into a sophisticated matching system that connects talent to opportunities based on deep qualitative alignment—something that has yet to be done successfully at scale"
""
"I'm thrilled to be collaborating with Statery founder Tejas Ravindra. His vision of guiding early-career talent towards roles aligned with their Ikigai—purpose-driven work at the intersection of passion, talent, and impact—resonates deeply with our mission at Careerspan."
""
"I would also be remiss if I didn't shout out the Careerspan and Startery dev teams for their effortless teamwork and delivery of a seamless product integration."
""
"We’re beyond excited. And we’re just getting started."
""
"#futureofwork #startups #careerspan #startery"
""
"[Press release in the comments]
voice_reason: Reads like a standard LinkedIn partnership announcement. Heavy on corporate enthusiasm clichés ('beyond excited,' 'just getting started,' 'thrilled to be collaborating,' 'sets off on a journey'). The hedging and inflated language ('precisely where Careerspan excels,' 'foundational,' 'sophisticated matching system') contradicts V's direct, no-corporate-speak style. A few flickers of substance (the Ikigai reference, the specific value prop about intangibles) but they're buried in formulaic LinkedIn-post structure. Almost anyone announcing a B2B partnership would write something similar.

text:
🎯 The Art of Personal Marketing: How to Stand Out in Your Job Applications"
""
"Ever wonder why some candidates get picked over others with similar qualifications? "
""
"Truth bomb time: Unique has nothing to do with it - it's all about presenting the RIGHT combination of your qualities!"
""
"Let me break down my framework for crafting a compelling personal narrative that *actually* gets noticed:"
""
"1. Strategic Selection 🎯"
"→ WHY? Because scattershotting everything you've got doesn't work!"
"• Focus on 5-7 of your strongest qualities"
"• Prioritize depth over spread"
"• Target what matters most for THIS role"
""
"2. Deep Analysis of the Job 📋"
"→ WHY? Different companies value different things, even for similar roles!"
"• Decode the job description (first listed = most important)"
"• Match your capabilities to their needs"
"• Look for hidden opportunities beyond the basics"
""
"3. Value Proposition Formula 💫"
"→ WHY? Listing skills isn't enough - you need to show their application!"
"Your Trait/Skill + How You Developed It + Their Impact = Winning Formula"
""
"4. Growth Mindset Integration 🌱"
"→ WHY? You don't need to be perfect - you just need to show potential!"
"• Show learning capability"
"• Demonstrate adaptability"
"• Present development journey"
""
"🔑 Pro Tip: Don't try to be everything to everyone. Better to shine brightly in a few areas than dimly across many!"
""
"Remember: Your uniqueness isn't in any single trait - it's in your specific combination of experiences, skills, and attributes. Package them strategically!"
""
"What's your biggest challenge in presenting yourself? Drop a comment below!"
""
"#JobSearch #CareerAdvice #PersonalBranding #JobApplications  #CareersWithVrijen #Careerspan
voice_reason: Reads like generic LinkedIn content marketing. Heavy emoji usage, 'Truth bomb time,' 'Drop a comment below,' and '#CareersWithVrijen' are formulaic social media patterns, not V's direct/specific voice. The framework itself has substance but is buried under engagement-bait formatting. The hedging-free assertiveness V is known for is replaced by exclamation-point energy. Almost no dry humor, no evidence-backed specificity, no distinctive rhythm — could be any career coach's LinkedIn post.

text:
Got another fascinating job recommendation today: Music Central Assistant at Endeavor."
""
"Their algorithm's logic? Flawless."
""
"I exist in New York. They exist in New York. I was once in a team. They have a team."
""
"My decade in career coaching? Irrelevant."
""
"My complete lack of passion for working in the music industry? Details!"
""
"This isn't even uniquely bad. LinkedIn once suggested I'd be "perfect" for entry-level social media."
""
"I can only assume this is because I'm below the age of 75 and don't live in a cave. "
""
"Every platform plays keyword bingo. "
""
""You're interested in Strategy, you say? Would you prefer Chief Strategy Officer at Land O' Lakes or SEO Strategy Specialist at Lockheed?" "
""
"What sucks is that Music Central Assistant is probably perfect for someone out there and they almost certainly didn't get this posting (though they definitely got the Lockheed one)."
""
"These platforms have no f*ckin clue who you are."
""
".....But what if they did?"
""
"Not just 50 keywords they forced you to vomit out during onboarding.  "
""
"I mean your values. Your deal-breakers. What makes YOU tick. And the context that informs whether you're applying to Disney or Deloitte. "
""
"That's why we're building Careerspan to be different. "
""
"We're coaching-driven because that is, objectively, the best way to understand a person and their goals--in all their messy and nuanced fullness. "
""
"So if you want a job recommendation system that gives enough of a shit to remember more than just your name, sign up at www.mycareerspan.com."
""
"Because the difference between matching keywords and matching humans makes all the difference in the world."
""
"Drop your best worst job rec below 👇
voice_reason: Textbook V voice. Opens with dry observational humor and specific absurd examples (Music Central Assistant, Land O'Lakes vs Lockheed). Builds argument through evidence and escalating specificity rather than abstract claims. The rhetorical structure—tear down the status quo with wit, then pivot to the pitch—is assertive and unhesitating. No hedging, no corporate speak. Profanity is deployed precisely for emphasis ('no f*ckin clue'), not gratuitously. The humor is bone-dry and observational ('I exist in New York. They exist in New York.'). Warmth comes through substance—genuine frustration on behalf of mismatched candidates, not performative empathy. The closing CTA is direct without being salesy. Strong, distinctive rhythm throughout with short punchy sentences alternating with longer explanatory ones. Excellent training signal.

text:
🎯 STOP Being Humble - It's Killing Your Career Chances! "
""
"Here's a cold, hard fact: Modesty isn't serving you in your job search. If anything, it's probably holding you back from landing your desired role."
""
"Here's the 3-step framework that transforms self-promotion from awkward to authentic:"
""
"1. Deep Introspection 🤔"
"→ WHY? Because you can't tell your story if you don't know it yourself!"
"- What core problems have you solved?"
"- How did you specifically tackle challenges?"
"- What measurable impact did you create?"
"- What unique lessons did you learn?"
""
"2. Strategic Packaging 📦"
"→ WHY? Because raw facts need context to create impact!"
"• Which achievements showcase your growth?"
"• Which experiences highlight your leadership or initiative-taking?"
"• What are indicators of the scale of your involvement or impact?"
""
"3. Confident Delivery 🎤"
"→ WHY? Because confidence without arrogance is the sweet spot!"
"→ Present your value without apologizing"
"→ Focus on facts, not just feelings"
"→ Share stories, not just statements"
""
"🚨 Pro Tip: Reflect on specific anecdotes from your life - they're easier to analyze than abstract questions like "What are my strengths?""
""
"Remember: If you won't advocate for yourself, who will? Your achievements aren't going to speak for themselves - YOU need to give them a voice! "
""
"Interested in knowing how to translate your newfound self-advocacy to landing your desired job? "
""
"Like and comment on this post, and I'll DM you when I post the follow-up to today's post: "🎯 The Art of Personal Marketing: How to Stand Out in Your Job Applications""
""
"#CareerAdvice #PersonalBranding #JobSearch #ProfessionalDevelopment #CareersWithVrijen #Careerspan
voice_reason: Heavy LinkedIn engagement-bait format (emoji headers, numbered framework, 'Like and comment' CTA, hashtag stack) drowns out any authentic voice. The tone is generic motivational coach — 'cold, hard fact,' 'sweet spot,' 'Pro Tip' — rather than direct and specific. No dry humor, no evidence-backed authority, no distinctive language choices. The rhetorical questions and exclamation marks read as corporate-inspirational, not warmth-through-substance. Could be written by any career content creator.

text:
Networking is *literally* the CEO's job."
""
"And by that measure, for over a year, I was the 0.1x founder"
""
""Too busy with product." "Locked down with fundraising." "Building the team comes first.""
"All true. All excuses."
""
"The real reason?"
"I didn't feel worthy of those rooms."
"Didn't feel worthy of introducing myself as a CEO."
"Didn't feel worthy of taking up space in conversations that mattered."
""
"I said what everyone says: "Networking feels so transactional.""
"What I really meant was: "I don't feel worthy of anyone's time.""
""
"Then, a few months ago, I forced myself to a (fantastic) breakfast organized by Andrew and the Fibe team."
""
"It was the best decision I've made in some time. "
""
"I was worried that it would be the 0.1x founder that showed up. "
""
"Instead, I threw myself into the deep end and found:"
"👉 Conversations that actually mattered."
"🤝 A partnership that re-energized our business. "
"👊 Connections that became real friends. "
""
"The difference?"
""
"After a year of building Careerspan, I finally knew our story."
"Not just knew it."
"I'd dissected it. Analyzed it. Internalized every bit of it."
""
"I could explain our vision in my sleep. "
"Our mission had become muscle memory. "
"Our impact was measured by an outpouring of user love. "
""
"Suddenly, talking about it was as easy as breathing."
"Because I wasn't selling."
"I was just... sharing what I knew to be true."
""
"So here's the lesson:"
"Your story matters."
"But first, you need to understand it so deeply that telling it comes naturally. "
""
"That's why we built Careerspan."
"🪏 To help you excavate your story. "
"📐 To understand it from every angle. "
"🧘‍♂️ To make sharing it as easy as breathing.
voice_reason: Textbook V voice. Vulnerability deployed as authority ('I didn't feel worthy'), not as weakness. Staccato rhythm with deliberate repetition ('Didn't feel worthy... Didn't feel worthy... Didn't feel worthy') creates cadence. Specificity earns trust — names Andrew and the Fibe team, uses '0.1x founder' as a precise, invented metric. The 'All true. All excuses.' line is peak dry directness. Reframes networking anxiety from generic platitude ('feels transactional') to honest confession ('I don't feel worthy of anyone's time') — warmth through substance. Closes with Careerspan tie-in that feels earned, not forced, because the personal story did the heavy lifting. Strong training signal for rhythm, vulnerability-as-authority, and specificity patterns.

text:
There are no end of articles like this one that all belabor the same basic point, and yet the point still doesn't seem to have gotten through to most people: Using ChatGPT poorly is just as bad for your job prospects - if not worse - than not using it at all. "
""
"People don't seem to get that AI isn't going to become magically creative on your behalf or figure out things about you out of the blue. "
""
"It is by definition constrained by what it knows (i. e. What you tell it). "
""
"What most people do is give it two pieces of info: the same job description as everyone else and their resume. Now granted everyone's resume is different, but as far as stores of information about you go, they aren't all that deep because all of your experiences are summarized into a dozen or so lines line. In the end, the bulk of what the AI has to draw from is still the JD. "
""
"So when you ask it to generate a cover letter or come up with new resume bullets, your stuff will come out sounding the same as everyone else's. "
""
"This problem is made worse when people give ChatGPT basic instructions that don't tell it what information to prioritize and or what structure to use, so it once again resorts to its base understanding of what to do. "
""
"The less context you give - for yourself, for your output, for your task - the more painfully generic the results. "
""
"For the record, that's why we've made discovery conversations with our AI career coach the centerpiece of our tool. To create truly great, personalized career content, you have to dig into your background in detail. That's what you need to do - whether that's by yourself, with a career coach, or using The ApplyAI. "
""
"#JobHunting #ChatGPT #Applying
voice_reason: Strong voice markers throughout: direct assertion ('point still doesn't seem to have gotten through'), specific mechanistic explanation of WHY generic AI output happens (same JD + shallow resume = same output), building an argument through evidence rather than just claiming authority. The 'stores of information about you go, they aren't all that deep' line is characteristically plain-spoken. Slight ding because the closing CTA paragraph shifts into a more promotional register ('for the record, that's why we've made...') and the hashtags are generic LinkedIn fare. But the core argument is specific, assertive, unhedged, and teaches through concrete reasoning — not platitudes. Good training signal for the explanatory-authoritative mode of V's voice.

text:
Humans use AI to spam applications."
"Companies use AI to filter out spam."
""
"It's a situation so tragic, Greek playwrights would call this "a bit much""
""
"So let me break it down Barney style for y'all. "
""
"Auto-apply is like me on the dance floor: it has a *very* limited set of moves and by jove is it going to use all of them."
""
"Swap "managed" for "spearheaded.""
"Guess that you "increased engagement by 25%." (why is it always 25%?)"
"Make up something so generic, the pharma industry wants to ban it"
""
"That's it. That's their whole schtick."
""
"It's as futile as trying to elevate a Ramen dinner."
"You can change the flavor packet."
"Add some frozen vegetables."
"Throw in some cheese if you're a nutcase like me."
"But when it's all said and done, that's still ramen."
""
"Because they don't really know you. And they don't care to as long as you'll pay them $50 a month and feel good that "number go up""
""
"Meanwhile, your *actual* career?"
"That's a walk-in fridge full of prime ingredients."
"The time you saved a product launch from disaster."
"Building a following from 500 to 5,000 during a brand crisis."
"The junior colleagues you mentored who all got promoted."
""
"Real stories. Real numbers. Real impact."
""
"Valuable context that auto-apply can't do jacksh*t without. "
""
"It's making instant ramen while your Michelin-worthy ingredients sit in the back--untouched, forgotten, and about to rot anytime. "
""
"Instead, it's the same, sad noodles hundreds of times a day."
"And hoping the hiring manager happens to be really, really hungry."
""
"So do yourself a massive favor: take stock of your career inventory."
""
"Because then, you'll really be cooking with gas!
voice_reason: Extremely distinctive voice throughout. The extended ramen metaphor is creative and committed, the self-deprecating humor ('a nutcase like me', 'me on the dance floor') is natural, the observational wit is sharp ('why is it always 25%?', Greek playwrights calling it 'a bit much'). Specific details (500 to 5,000, $50 a month) earn authority. 'Barney style', 'jacksh*t', 'number go up' — the register shifts are deliberate and confident, never hedging. Direct assertions without qualifiers. The rhythm alternates between punchy one-liners and flowing analogies. This is unmistakably one person's voice — warm, assertive, funny, and specific. Excellent training signal.

text:
🎯 The *Real* Strategic Networker's Workflow (Because Random Connection Requests Don't Work!)"
""
"I've reviewed hundreds of networking approaches, and here's the uncomfortable truth: Most professionals are sabotaging relationships before they even start."
""
"Why? Because they treat networking like a numbers game instead of a strategic investment."
""
"Here’s what you should be doing:"
""
"1.⁠ ⁠Collect Vital Intel🔍"
"(Because surface-level research = surface-level relationships)"
"•⁠ ⁠Map your targeting strategically"
"•⁠ ⁠Study their career trajectory"
"•⁠ ⁠Identify genuine conversation starters"
"•⁠ ⁠Understand their industry challenges"
""
"2.⁠ ⁠Open with Confidence 💫"
"(First impressions last forever in professional relationships)"
"•⁠ ⁠Design outreach that proves you've done homework"
"•⁠ ⁠Reference specific work impacts"
"•⁠ ⁠Demonstrate informed curiosity"
"•⁠ ⁠Keep your ask small and specific"
""
"3.⁠ ⁠Cultivate a Relationship 🌱"
"(Value first, ask later - this is where trust is built)"
"•⁠ ⁠Position yourself as an engaged learner"
"•⁠ ⁠Share relevant industry insights"
"•⁠ ⁠Maintain meaningful touchpoints"
"•⁠ ⁠Focus on mutual growth"
""
"⚠️ Key Insight: Rushing to ask for favors before building rapport kills professional relationships, period. "
""
"Think about it: Would YOU help someone who clearly hasn't taken time to understand your work?"
""
"What aspects of networking feel particularly challenging? "
""
"Would sharing some real-life examples or critiquing past messages from real people be helpful?"
""
"Share and comment below for more such content!"
""
"#StrategicNetworking #ProfessionalGrowth #CareerDevelopment #NetworkingTips #CareerspanCareerspanCareerspan
voice_reason: Reads like generic LinkedIn content marketing. Heavy emoji use, listicle format with vague bullet points ('maintain meaningful touchpoints', 'focus on mutual growth'), engagement-bait closing ('Share and comment below'), and hashtag stacking are all formulaic LinkedIn playbook moves. The parenthetical asides try to add personality but feel templated. No specific evidence, no real examples, no dry humor — just assertive-sounding generalities. The 'uncomfortable truth' opener is a common LinkedIn hook pattern. V's voice earns authority through specificity and evidence; this piece makes claims ('I've reviewed hundreds') without delivering substance to back them up.

text:
🚨 Here's a fact: Your dream hire is probably terrible at applying for jobs."
""
"Think about it."
""
"That 10x engineer? Been coding for 5 years straight. Last updated their resume in 2019."
""
"That unicorn marketer? Too busy tripling revenue to practice interview answers."
""
"Your perfect candidate is crushing it at work—not polishing their LinkedIn weekly."
""
"Unfortunately, self-advocacy is a muscle. Use it or lose it."
""
"And when you're genuinely good at your job, you don't exercise that muscle. "
""
"You're too busy... being good at your job."
""
"Yet somehow, we've created a hiring system that rewards job-search practice over job performance."
""
"So how do we fix this?"
""
"The SAT gives everyone free prep materials. "
"Not to make it easier, but to make it fair. "
"To measure what actually matters--relative performance on equal footing. "
""
"🤔 Why don't we do this for hiring?"
""
"Why don't we help people put forward their best selves?"
""
"This isn't about charity—it's about meritocracy."
""
"When every candidate can articulate their value clearly, you finally get to compare apples to apples. "
""
"Real capability vs. real capability. "
"Not "who hired a resume writer" vs. "who didn't.""
""
"At Careerspan, we help candidates uncover their real stories through AI-guided reflection. "
"No BS templates. "
"Just structured conversations that reveal who they actually are."
""
"👉 Because when you help candidates shine, you can see who shines brightest."
""
"And right now? "
"You're not rejecting bad candidates. "
"🤦 You're rejecting bad self-advocates that might be your next superstar hire. "
""
"If you're ready to fix this for your organization, let's connect!
voice_reason: Strong voice signal. The core argument is specific and evidence-based (SAT analogy, concrete examples like '10x engineer' and 'unicorn marketer'), which fits V's substance-over-fluff style. The rhythm is punchy and assertive—short declarative sentences, no hedging. The 'self-advocacy is a muscle' throughline is a distinctive framing device. Docked from 5 because: the emoji usage (🚨🤔🤦👉) leans LinkedIn-native in a way that slightly dilutes authenticity, the closing CTA ('let's connect!') is standard LinkedIn boilerplate, and a few lines trend toward motivational-poster cadence rather than V's drier observational tone. But the argument structure—building a logical case with specifics, then flipping the frame ('you're not rejecting bad candidates')—is distinctly V.

text:
I'm thrilled to announce that Valerie Sutton is a Careerspan advisor!"
""
"From our very first conversation, I knew she was the perfect fit. Why? Because she's a genuine career geek (just like Logan Currie and me) who doesn't just love helping people advance their careers – she's passionate about the craft itself."
""
"It's that commitment to helping individuals discover career paths that genuinely align with who they are in clever and inventive ways that makes Valerie truly special, and why her influence has already been transformative for us:"
"- She's brought an invaluable POV as someone with decades in the game"
"- She's inspired us to maintain our unique approach while staying focused on real impact"
"- She's brought a balance of philosophy and practicality that's helped us drive novel feature development"
""
"Valerie's own words say it best: she's dedicated to helping professionals develop "career consciousness". Her belief that careers span transitions and transformations aligns perfectly with our mission. What excites me most is how her data-informed approach bridges academia and industry – precisely what we need to help professionals navigate today's increasingly complex work landscape."
""
"We couldn't be prouder to welcome Valerie to the Careerspan team as we continue building tools that help people document their experiences and craft evolving career narratives."
""
"What career transition have you found most challenging to articulate to others? Tell me about them in the comments! The stories we share about our professional journeys matter more than we realize."
""
"#CareerTransitions #CareerDevelopment #ProfessionalGrowth #CareerCoaching
voice_reason: Opens with 'I'm thrilled to announce' — classic LinkedIn corporate speak V's voice profile explicitly avoids. Heavy on superlatives ('invaluable', 'truly special', 'transformative', 'couldn't be prouder') that feel formulaic rather than earned through specificity. The bullet points gesture at substance but stay vague ('decades in the game', 'unique approach', 'real impact'). The closing engagement-bait question and hashtag stack are generic LinkedIn playbook. Some flickers of V's actual interests (career consciousness, data-informed approach, evolving narratives) but they're buried under a thick layer of announcement-post template language. Weak training signal — teaching the model to write like LinkedIn, not like V.

text:
This just in: Anthropic (yes, that one) is telling job applicants not to use AI tools in their applications. "
""
"Let me play that back just in case you missed that."
""
"An AI company that's actively working towards a future where AI transforms everything we do... doesn't want AI to transform applying to jobs… At their AI company. You can't make this up! "
""
"Here's what I find puzzling:"
"- They say AI will revolutionize work (except, apparently, for their own hiring process?)"
"- They say their ideal candidate will “be good to users” but then want said candidates to compete with one arm tied behind their backs."
"- They claim "AI-assisted communication" is unacceptable, but have left it so vaguely defined that applicants are left guessing where the line is."
""
"Here's the thing - as someone working in AI-enabled career tech myself, I *get* where the concerns are coming from. The Wild West of AI-generated applications isn't ideal for anyone."
""
"However, instead of a blanket "AI bad" policy, I believe the leading AI alignment company should do just that – lead on this issue by:"
"- Developing robust, AI-resistant hiring practices"
"- Acknowledging that purely "unassisted communication" is quickly becoming a relic of the past"
"- Establishing clear guidelines for what types of AI assistance are acceptable"
"- Pioneering best practices for ethical AI use in hiring"
""
"The real opportunity here isn't to ban AI outright - it's to lead the way in demonstrating what the responsible, effective use of AI in hiring looks like. "
""
"Let's go, Anthropic - light the way forward for all of us!"
""
"#AIHiring #FutureOfWork #Anthropic #Careerspan
voice_reason: Strong voice markers: the opening hook is punchy and direct, the irony is laid out with dry observational humor ('You can't make this up!'), and the pivot to constructive critique shows warmth-through-substance. The 'let me play that back' device and the specific bullet-point takedowns are distinctive. Docked from 5 because the LinkedIn post format introduces some formulaic elements — the hashtags, the 'Here's the thing' transition, and the rallying closer ('Let's go, Anthropic') lean slightly toward platform convention rather than purely unique voice. But the core argument is specific, assertive without hedging, and earns authority by naming the contradiction precisely rather than just opining.

text:
The AI job application "arms race" is perpetuating a hiring doom loop that hurts EVERYONE."
""
"I just read a fascinating piece (link in the comments) by Michael Horn and Bob Moesta that perfectly captures what I've been seeing in the job market - we're trapped in a vicious cycle where AI tools are making things WORSE, not better."
""
"Here's the toxic pattern they've identified:"
"• Job seekers use AI to blast out hundreds of generic applications"
"• Employers get flooded with thousands of AI-generated resumes"
"• Companies either double-down on their own AI screening or abandon it entirely"
"• Hiring managers retreat to the safety of personal referrals"
"• Qualified candidates without connections get left behind"
""
"It's essentially mutually assured destruction for both sides of the hiring equation."
""
"In my opinion, the current generation of candidate-side AI tools are primarily fueling this problem because they focus on brute-force volume rather than meaningful personalization. "
""
"LazyApply, JobCopilot and their ilk are selling false hope - tricking job seekers into thinking they're making progress when they're actually just contributing to the noise."
""
"But here's where I think Careertech founders need to listen up: We need AI tools with INTEGRITY. Not tools that help you spam 500 employers, but tools that help you create genuinely tailored, authentic applications for roles you're actually qualified for."
""
"Until then, I'm with Horn and Moesta - invest your time in building real relationships instead of feeding the application machine."
""
"#AIHiring #JobSearch #NetworkingMatters #CareerTech
voice_reason: Reads like a standard LinkedIn thought-leadership post. Heavy on performative outrage (ALL-CAPS emphasis, 'doom loop,' 'mutually assured destruction'), formulaic structure (bullet list → hot take → call to action → hashtags), and hedging phrases ('In my opinion,' 'I think') that contradict V's assertive-without-hedging style. The reference to Horn/Moesta shows some specificity, but the framing ('fascinating piece,' 'perfectly captures') is generic LinkedIn praise. 'Here's where I think Careertech founders need to listen up' is corporate-influencer cadence, not V's direct warmth-through-substance voice. Hashtags and the overall template (share article → add commentary → CTA) are formulaic. Weak training signal — this could be any career-space LinkedIn poster.

text:
The AI job application "arms race" is perpetuating a hiring doom loop that hurts EVERYONE."
""
"I just read a fascinating piece (link in the comments) by Michael Horn and Bob Moesta that perfectly captures what I've been seeing in the job market - we're trapped in a vicious cycle where AI tools are making things WORSE, not better."
""
"Here's the toxic pattern they've identified:"
"• Job seekers use AI to blast out hundreds of generic applications"
"• Employers get flooded with thousands of AI-generated resumes"
"• Companies either double-down on their own AI screening or abandon it entirely"
"• Hiring managers retreat to the safety of personal referrals"
"• Qualified candidates without connections get left behind"
""
"It's essentially mutually assured destruction for both sides of the hiring equation."
""
"In my opinion, the current generation of candidate-side AI tools are primarily fueling this problem because they focus on brute-force volume rather than meaningful personalization. "
""
"LazyApply, JobCopilot and their ilk are selling false hope - tricking job seekers into thinking they're making progress when they're actually just contributing to the noise."
""
"But here's where I think Careertech founders need to listen up: We need AI tools with INTEGRITY. Not tools that help you spam 500 employers, but tools that help you create genuinely tailored, authentic applications for roles you're actually qualified for."
""
"Until then, I'm with Horn and Moesta - invest your time in building real relationships instead of feeding the application machine."
""
"#AIHiring #JobSearch #NetworkingMatters #CareerTech
voice_reason: Reads like a standard LinkedIn thought-leader post. Heavy reliance on formulaic structures (bullet lists, 'Here's the thing', hashtags), hedging phrases ('In my opinion', 'I think'), and generic calls-to-action. The ALL-CAPS emphasis and 'listen up' feel performative rather than naturally assertive. References others' work more than demonstrating original insight. The voice could belong to any career-space LinkedIn poster — lacks the dry humor, specificity, and substance-over-signaling that define V's authentic voice.

text:
This shocks … no one. "
""
"The standout quote to me? "
""
"“Companies are swamped by applicants in part because many job seekers are newly relying on ChatGPT and automated bots to fire off large volumes of résumés, or using other shortcuts like LinkedIn’s “easy apply” button. ” "
""
"Basically: as a bad system gets worse, social capital becomes more important than ever. "
""
"Why am I worried for people?"
"- Networking is tough enough as it is - neurodiverse candidates, introverts, and people outside of traditionally “connected” circles will have it worst of all though"
"- The bar for getting a referral will inevitably rise. At McKinsey, I was asked a lot of questions about the person I was referring to the point where, if I barely knew someone or didn't have relevant info about them on-hand, the referral would have been buried by the system immediately"
""
"Luckily, the best solution is a simple one: double down on authenticity. "
""
"Take the time to understand your value proposition and practice communicating it to people who can help you in sufficient detail.  They'll need the ammo if you want them to fight on your behalf!"
""
"It's as simple as that.  "
""
"Don't rely on ChatGPT to do the heavy lifting - you'll spend just as much time trying to tease out something natural-sounding as you would have if you did it yourself, but without the added benefit of developing an increasingly important career skill. "
""
"That's why applicants that find success in this job market will excel at two things:"
"#1 Being "referral ready" at all times"
"#2 Being easy to refer on effectively"
""
"That’s the new bar.
voice_reason: Highly characteristic. Opens with dry, punchy humor ('shocks … no one'). Pulls a specific quote as evidence, then reframes it in his own assertive language ('Basically: as a bad system gets worse...'). Shows warmth-through-substance by naming specific vulnerable groups (neurodiverse, introverts). Uses a concrete personal anecdote (McKinsey referral process) to earn authority. No hedging — 'It's as simple as that.' Direct calls to action with specific frameworks (#1, #2). The 'ammo' and 'fight on your behalf' metaphors are distinctly his. Anti-corporate-speak stance is explicit ('Don't rely on ChatGPT to do the heavy lifting'). Strong training signal across rhythm, specificity, and personality.

text:
The ever-incisive Rana el Kaliouby, Ph.D. gets it exactly right about career transitions and AI's role in professional development. As someone building in Careertech, I'm seeing firsthand how careers have become deeply tied to identity and happiness - just like our health. Today, when you’re making a career switch, you’re putting all of that on the line in one of the hardest job markets in history. "
""
"And the reality is dire: career transitions are more complex than ever, and the market has zero patience for unfocused career changers. Employers demand clear value articulation from everyone, putting career switchers at a disadvantage, which is why we've focused intensely on AI-facilitated career development at Careerspan."
""
"What resonates in Rana's perspective is her emphasis on being thoughtful about one's transitions. Her path from academia to entrepreneurship to VC shows the power of intentional career management. The key isn't the transition itself - it's making it strategically."
""
"This is where AI becomes transformative: not as a basic tool, but as a sophisticated system fostering self-reflection, skill mapping, and strategic planning. We're turning Generative AI into Facilitative AI, augmenting rather than replacing human insight. "
""
"For a deeper dive into our POV, check out Logan Currie's discussion with Rana on the Pioneers of AI podcast (link in comments)!"
""
"Making a career pivot? What's your biggest challenge? Share below."
""
"#CareerDevelopment #AIinCareers #CareerTransitions #FutureOfWork #ProfessionalDevelopment
voice_reason: Has some genuine voice markers — 'zero patience for unfocused career changers' is sharp and specific, and the Generative→Facilitative AI framing shows real thought. But it's diluted by LinkedIn-post conventions: the flattering opener ('ever-incisive'), the engagement-bait closer ('Share below'), the hashtag stack, and phrases like 'transformative' and 'deeper dive' that read as platform-generic. The middle paragraphs earn authority through specificity (market reality, employer demands), which is on-voice, but the bookends are formulaic enough to weaken the overall signal. Decent training material if trimmed of the LinkedIn scaffolding.

text:
Today, I took #Manus for a spin with a straightforward goal: sourcing recent job descriptions fitting certain criteria for a new beta feature at Careerspan."
""
"I instructed it to look up several job descriptions, copy them verbatim into a text file, and include URLs. Initially, everything appeared great—the process was quick, and the output seemed clean."
""
"But then, that little voice inside my head (not my conscience, but rather my CTO/Head of AI, Ilse Funkhouser) reminded me not to blindly trust AI. "
""
"So, I ran a quick validation check, and thank goodness I did! Key details were missing, and new, inaccurate information had been introduced. What seemed like a straightforward, accurate result was anything but!"
""
"This experience was an important reminder that:"
"1. Tasks we humans view as simple or rote aren't necessarily straightforward for AI"
"2. Output that looks right isn't necessarily output that *is* right"
"3. Generative AI is inherently probabilistic, and without guardrails to ensure accuracy and reliability, you run the risk of flawed results"
"4. Even with guardrails, there's no harm in double-checking your output on mission-critical tasks "
""
"This isn't a post meant to hate on Manus - it's is a pretty awesome tool worth trying - but rather a reminder to not put all your faith in AI!"
""
"On a side note, experiences like this reinforce my gratitude for the meticulous standards we've set at Careerspan. A big shout-out to Ilse for keeping us sharp, vigilant, and safe from the pitfalls of AI hallucinations!
voice_reason: Has some authentic V markers — the specific Ilse callout, the parenthetical humor ('not my conscience, but rather my CTO'), and the Careerspan context. But it's diluted by LinkedIn-generic patterns: the numbered listicle of AI truisms, the diplomatic 'this isn't a post meant to hate on' hedge, the exclamation marks, and the 'big shout-out' closing. The takeaways read like anyone's AI caution post. The opening is strong and specific; the back half drifts into safe, crowd-pleasing territory that weakens the voice signal.

text:
🎣 You know that saying about teaching someone to fish? That's exactly how I feel about AI prompting."
""
"When you learn to craft effective prompts, you unlock AI's ability to enhance not just what you can do, but what you thought was possible."
""
"Yet in recent conversations with people about how they're using AI to advance their careers, I've consistently noticed that many of us aren't getting the most out of our AI interactions. "
""
"So today, I'm sharing something practical: a prompt builder that helps you craft high-quality AI prompts for your daily workflows."
""
"Two ways to access:"
"1. Custom GPT version (for ChatGPT Pro or Plus users)"
"2. PDF version (works with any LLM - and even on free plans)"
""
"Pro tip: For best results, use reasoning models:"
"ChatGPT: o series models i.e. o3, o4, or o1"
"Claude: 3.7 with extended thinking enabled"
""
"You can access the custom GPT here: https://lnkd.in/eF3bBMXe"
""
"You can access the raw function file here: https://lnkd.in/exkQiXXX "
"Use this by:"
"1) Uploading this PDF into a conversation with your LLM of choice"
"2) Providing some initial context for the prompt you want to generate and asking it to run this function to help build it"
""
"If you try it out, I'd love to hear how the prompts you build compare to your previous ones. Drop your examples in the comments!"
""
"Like and share if you want me share more custom GPTs/prompts that can boost your productivity!"
""
"#AIProductivity #PromptEngineering #Careerspan
voice_reason: Mostly generic LinkedIn post formula: hook → value prop → CTA → engagement bait. The fishing metaphor opener is cliché, 'unlock AI's ability to enhance' is corporate filler, and 'Drop your examples in the comments!' / 'Like and share' are standard engagement templates. Minor voice flickers in the practical specificity of model recommendations (o3, o4, Claude 3.7 with extended thinking), but the overall structure and language could be any LinkedIn creator. No dry humor, no assertive stance, no evidence-backed authority — just a resource share wrapped in formulaic framing.

text:
I wanted to share this brilliant piece from our own Ilse Funkhouser that perfectly articulates what we've been building together at Careerspan. Her guitarist/surgeon analogy captures the essence of our shared philosophy: professional value is never absolute - it only emerges when viewed through the right contextual lens."
""
"This context-driven approach has been central to our work from day one. We recognized that traditional career development fails precisely because it treats skills as universal constants rather than contextual variables. We've built our platform around this insight, creating a system that captures rich narratives that can be dynamically reinterpreted as career goals evolve."
""
"What makes me particularly proud of our approach is how it removes unnecessary burdens from job seekers: We don't expect our users to become experts in self-reflection or experience translation - that's what we're here for. Instead, we do the heavy lifting of contextual interpretation so they can focus on confidently presenting their authentic selves."
""
"I love the way she's phrased the end goal: "this doesn't just make your job search easier—it makes it more human." "
""
"This human-centered approach is what differentiates Careerspan in a marketplace filled with generic career tools."
""
"What professional experiences have you found gain new meaning when viewed through a different contextual lens?"
""
"#CareerDevelopment #TeamCareerspan #ProfessionalGrowth
voice_reason: Reads like a polished LinkedIn reshare post with heavy corporate-speak ('human-centered approach,' 'differentiates Careerspan in a marketplace,' 'contextual variables'). The engagement question at the end is textbook LinkedIn formula. Hedging and promotional tone throughout — 'What makes me particularly proud,' 'I wanted to share this brilliant piece' — is warm but generic founder-boosting-team content. Very little specificity, no dry humor, no assertive directness. The one authentic moment is quoting Ilse's line, but the surrounding commentary smooths out any personality. Could be any founder resharing a team member's post.

text:
Hard skills are table stakes."
""
"That’s right, I said it."
""
"46% of new hires fail within 18 months."
"But here's the kicker - it's NOT because they can't do the job."
"We're actually pretty good at validating technical competencies."
""
"90% of why these failures are because of a values, mindset, or soft skills misalignment."
"That’s what actually matters and we are TERRIBLE at assessing it"
""
"Every employer can verify if someone can code, analyze data, or manage projects."
"That's the easy part."
""
"What we can't figure out (at scale) is:"
"⚫️ Will they thrive in our specific chaos?"
"⚫️ Do their values align with how we actually work?"
"⚫️ Can they adapt when everything changes next quarter?"
""
"The best companies know this."
"They run extensive interviews, behavioral assessments, culture deep-dives."
"It works - but it doesn't scale."
"And it’s hard as hell - which is why most interview processes are 10 stages long and are run like F train on St. Paddy’s Day."
""
"In an AI-augmented world, hard skills will become EVEN MORE commoditized."
"Ironically, what will matter the most is what we’re worst at measuring."
""
"At Careerspan, we're working hard on solving this exact problem for companies, and we’re doing so in a truly human-centric way"
"Our customers are seeing clear dividends: fewer screenings, fewer interview phases, more confident hiring decisions."
""
"If you keep wondering why your "perfect on paper" hires keep failing, reach out!
voice_reason: Strong voice markers throughout: assertive opening ('That's right, I said it'), specific stats used as evidence (46%, 90%), dry humor ('run like F train on St. Paddy's Day'), direct challenge to reader without hedging. The CAPS for emphasis ('NOT', 'TERRIBLE', 'EVEN MORE') and rhetorical structure show personality. Docked from 5 because the closing CTA ('reach out!') dips into generic LinkedIn-post territory, and 'working hard on solving' / 'truly human-centric way' lean slightly corporate. But the overall rhythm — short punchy lines, building an argument through specificity, warmth earned through real insight rather than pleasantries — is distinctly V.
